name: Arnotegi Hermitage Loop from Puente la Reina
name_original: Ruta de los Fuertes – circular desde Puente la Reina/Gares
description: Hike the easy 3.9-mile Route of the Forts from Puente la Reina/Gares, exploring historical strongholds and enjoying panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Puente La Reina/Gares offers diverse landscapes in the Valdizarbe Valley, where the Arga and Robo rivers converge. The terrain features vineyards, olive groves, and rolling hills, with views of the Navarre countryside. Hikers can expect paths through pine, poplar, oak, and birch forests, alongside riverside trails. The region is a key point on the Camino de Santiago, providing a network of established routes. Best hiking trails around Puente La Reina/Gares The most popular hiking route is Arnotegi Hermitage Loop from Puente la Reina, a 4.0 miles (6.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 50 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the area around the historic hermitage. Another top favourite among local hikers is Arnotegi Hermitage – Pilgrim Monument loop from Puente la Reina / Gares, a moderate 7.6 miles (12.2 km) path. This route connects the hermitage with a pilgrim monument, offering varied scenery. Local hikers also love the Puente la Reina Bridge – Pilgrim Monument loop from Puente la Reina / Gares, a 5.5 miles (8.9 km) trail leading through the historic town and towards the pilgrim monument, often completed in about 2 hours 24 minutes. Hiking around Puente La Reina/Gares is defined by vineyards, olive groves, rolling hills, and riverside forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging paths. The routes in Puente La Reina/Gares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 60 reviews. More than 500 hikers have used komoot to explore Puente La Reina/Gares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking around Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The trails in Puente La Reina/Gares traverse diverse landscapes. You'll encounter picturesque vineyards, olive groves, and rolling hills offering wide views of the Navarre countryside. Many routes also lead through forests of pines, poplars, oaks, and birches, and along the tranquil Arga River, where lush riverside forests provide a different natural setting.

Q: Are there hiking trails suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy walk?
A: Yes, Puente La Reina/Gares offers several easy hiking options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970225129/arnotegi-hermitage-loop-from-puente-la-reina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arnotegi Hermitage Loop from Puente la Reina&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.0-mile (6.4 km) trail that explores the area around the historic hermitage. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10517542" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente la Reina Bridge – Calle de Puente la Reina loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 3.6 miles (5.8 km) and also rated easy.

Q: What historical landmarks or points of interest can I discover on the trails?
A: Puente La Reina/Gares is rich in history, especially as a key point on the Camino de Santiago. You can explore the iconic 11th-century Romanesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/119191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, which is a central landmark for many walks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/654847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pilgrim Monument at Alto del Perdón&lt;/a&gt; offers panoramic views and is a significant stop on the Camino. Additionally, the unique octagonal &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/398800" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María de Eunate&lt;/a&gt;, known for its Mozarabic influences, is located nearby and is a fascinating site to visit.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, many of the trails around Puente La Reina/Gares are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Popular circular routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970225129/arnotegi-hermitage-loop-from-puente-la-reina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arnotegi Hermitage Loop from Puente la Reina&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1144029" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arnotegi Hermitage – Pilgrim Monument loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers varied scenery. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31865173" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Scenic Ascent Near Pamplona – Maneru loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The region is enjoyable for hiking across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural colors, with vineyards and olive groves at their best. Summer can be warm, but riverside trails provide cooler options. The Camino de Santiago routes are popular year-round, though some sections might be more challenging in winter conditions.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails?
A: Generally, many trails in the Puente La Reina/Gares area are dog-friendly, especially those through natural landscapes like vineyards, olive groves, and forests. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially near livestock or in populated areas, and to check specific trail regulations if you plan to enter protected areas or private land.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options?
A: Yes, the area offers trails suitable for families. The easier, shorter loops, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970225129/arnotegi-hermitage-loop-from-puente-la-reina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arnotegi Hermitage Loop&lt;/a&gt;, are great for families with children. These routes often feature relatively flat terrain and interesting sights, making them enjoyable for all ages.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The hiking routes in Puente La Reina/Gares are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 60 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, from vineyards and olive groves to riverside paths, and the historical significance of the Camino de Santiago routes. The well-maintained paths and scenic viewpoints are also frequently highlighted.

Q: How many hiking trails are there in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: There are over 18 hiking routes available in Puente La Reina/Gares, catering to various skill levels. This includes 8 easy routes, 8 moderate routes, and 2 more challenging options, ensuring there's something for every hiker.

Q: Is there public transport available to reach the trailheads?
A: Puente La Reina/Gares is a well-connected town, particularly due to its position on the Camino de Santiago. Local and regional bus services connect the town to nearby cities like Pamplona, which can serve as a starting point for accessing various trailheads. For detailed schedules and routes, it's advisable to check local transport information or consult resources like &lt;a href="https://visitpuentelareinagares.com/en/puente-la-reina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;visitpuentelareinagares.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find parking when hiking in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: As a popular stop on the Camino de Santiago, Puente La Reina/Gares offers various parking options, especially near the town center and main attractions. There are often public parking areas available that are convenient for accessing trailheads that start directly from the town. It's recommended to look for designated parking zones upon arrival.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, while many routes are easy to moderate, there are also more challenging options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15001347" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente la Reina Bridge – Pilgrim Monument loop&lt;/a&gt;, though moderate in distance, is rated as difficult due to its terrain. The region's varied topography, including sections of the Camino de Santiago, can offer steeper climbs and more demanding paths for those seeking a greater challenge.
tips: The ruins of this ancient fortification are very close to the Arnotegi hill and offer very beautiful views of the Rabo river valley and the town of Puente la Reina.
It is a pleasure to visit this "hermitage/fort". Very recommendable.

This place of worship, which is named after the Holy Cross (Latin "Santa Croce"), relates its patronage to the cross on which Jesus Christ died. The veneration of the Holy Cross goes back in large part to Saint Helena, who was the mother of Emperor Constantine the Great (who, after his victory at the "Milvian Bridge" outside Rome, recognized Christianity as a religion and in 312 the long time of persecution ended with the Milan “Tolerance Edict”). In the early fourth century she undertook a pilgrimage to the Holy Land (Israel / Palestine) and brought numerous relics from there to Europe, including the "True Cross" on which Jesus Christ was crucified. Helena is therefore often depicted with a cross as an attribute. The feast of the discovery, public presentation and veneration of the Holy Cross - called "Exaltation of the Cross" - is celebrated by the Church on September 14th.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Forts suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally considered easy, making it suitable for all fitness levels and skill sets. Its accessible paths make it a good option for a leisurely hike with family.

faqs.question: How long does the Route of the Forts hike typically take?
faqs.answer: The trail covers approximately 6.36 kilometers (around 4 miles) and typically takes about 1 hour and 51 minutes to complete.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Route of the Forts?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring ascents to historical strongholds and pleasant stretches along the Arga River. You'll experience inclines to forts like Fuerte Infanta Isabel, the highest point, followed by descents past landmarks such as the Arnotegi Hermitage and the Church of the Crucifix.

faqs.question: What historical sites and viewpoints can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route visits remnants of several Carlist forts, including Fuerte de San Gregorio and Argurutz / Fort of Infanta Isabel. You'll also find excellent panoramic views from Montículo El Reducto and the View of the countryside from the Hermitage of Our Lady of Arnotegui.

faqs.question: Where does the Route of the Forts trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail is a circular route that typically starts and ends at the Romanesque bridge in Puente la Reina/Gares. A panel there provides maps and details. Parking is generally available within Puente la Reina/Gares, though specific parking areas are not detailed in the trail information.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Forts trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail is generally suitable for dogs, but it's always recommended to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas or near historical sites, and to respect local regulations.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike the Route of the Forts?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round due to its easy difficulty. However, spring and autumn offer milder temperatures and pleasant scenery, making them ideal for hiking in the Navarra region.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to hike the Route of the Forts. It is a publicly accessible trail.

faqs.question: How well is the Route of the Forts trail marked?
faqs.answer: The trail is officially homologated by the Navarra Mountain Federation, ensuring its quality and marking. It is well-signaled and features informative signs at various points of interest, including the forts and a panoramic table at Montículo El Reducto.

faqs.question: Does the Route of the Forts intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other official trails. You will encounter parts of the Camino de la Vera Cruz (El camino de los Templarios), Camino Francés - 01, and the Aragonese Way, among others.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or amenities near the trail?
faqs.answer: Puente la Reina/Gares, the starting and ending point of the circular route, offers various amenities such as cafes, pubs, and accommodation options for hikers.
